did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning for feature extraction (deep features) and rotation-invariant Local Binary Pattern (RI-LBP) for handcrafted feature extraction from dermoscopic images. These combined features are used to train an XGBoost machine learning classifier to perform skin cancer (melanoma) classification, achieving an AUC of 0.91.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is published in the '2023 IEEE Pune Section International Conference (PuneCon)' and explicitly states in the abstract: 'This research paper introduces a novel technique for automated melanoma classification'. It presents original experimental work involving a proposed methodology combining deep features with RI-LBP features, training an XGBoost classifier on a specific dataset (ISIC2019), and reporting new performance metrics (AUC of 0.91, accuracy of 0.85). Although it includes a literature survey section, its primary purpose is to report original research results rather than summarize prior literature.

title: Combining Deep features with the Invariant Local Binary Pattern features for Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated melanoma classification by combining deep features with rotation-invariant Local Binary Pattern (RI-LBP) features. The researchers utilized the ISIC2019 dataset, which contained 3,297 dermoscopic images of benign and malignant skin lesions, split into training and test sets. Deep features were extracted using transfer learning, while RI-LBP captured local microstructures, and these combined feature vectors were used to train an XGBoost machine learning classifier. The model's performance was evaluated against other machine learning algorithms using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and Area Under the Curve (AUC). The proposed method achieved an AUC of 0.91 and an accuracy of 0.85, demonstrating its efficacy in distinguishing between malignant melanomas and benign lesions. The results suggest that this combined feature approach provides a reliable and accurate tool to aid dermatologists in skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification, representing malignant skin lesions. The study distinguishes melanoma from benign lesions using the ISIC2019 dataset, which contained 1,497 images belonging to the malignant class.
The comparator class for the binary classification task. The ISIC2019 dataset contained 1,800 images belonging to the benign class. The model aims to differentiate these from malignant melanomas.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign skin lesions using the ISIC2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using an XGBoost classifier trained on combined deep and RI-LBP features. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset derived from the ISIC2019 archives, consisting of dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used to train the XGBoost machine learning classifier with combined deep and RI-LBP features.
use.testing: Used to assess the performance of the trained models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

ISIC2019.description: Dataset split into training (2,637 images) and testing (660 images) sets in a 0.8:0.2 ratio. Classes: Benign (1,800 images) and Malignant (1,497 images).

Comparison of Feature Descriptors with S.note: Accuracy scores for different feature descriptors combined with three classifiers.

Performance of Classifiers with Combined.note: Detailed metrics for classifiers trained on the combined feature set.

Comparison with Related Works.note: Comparison of the proposed model against previous studies cited in the paper.

ISIC2019.notes: The XGBoost classifier with combined deep and RI-LBP features achieved the best performance with an AUC of 0.91 and Accuracy of 0.85. The Random Forest classifier also achieved an AUC of 0.91 but with lower accuracy (0.83) compared to XGBoost.

architecture_used.pretraining: Deep features were extracted from pre-trained CNNs (ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, and DenseNet121) using transfer learning.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Combined deep features and rotation-invariant Local Binary Pattern (RI-LBP) features. Deep features were obtained by passing feature maps from pre-trained CNNs through a global average pooling layer. RI-LBP features were extracted with radius r=1 and neighborhood pixels p=8, resulting in 9 uniform patterns.
architecture_details.feature_combination: Deep features and RI-LBP features were combined into a single feature vector.

training_details.split: Training set: 2,637 images; Test set: 660 images (0.8:0.2 ratio).
training_details.preprocessing: Reshaping to (64, 64, 3) and normalization by standardization (subtracting mean and dividing by standard deviation).

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization by standardization (subtracting the mean and dividing pixel values by the standard deviation)
conclusions: Combining deep features with rotation-invariant Local Binary Pattern (RI-LBP) features positively impacts the performance of skin cancer classification into benign and malignant classes.
The proposed method, which combines deep features and RI-LBP features, acts as a regularizer to prevent overfitting that may arise due to the limited availability of the dataset.
Deep features capture high-level abstract details, while RI-LBP captures local microstructures; these feature types complement each other when combined.
The XGBoost classifier trained on the combined deep and RI-LBP features achieved the best performance among the tested classifiers, with an accuracy of 0.85 and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.91.

The proposed solution is considered reliable and accurate for categorizing dermoscopic images into benign and malignant classes, suitable for aiding dermatologists in enhancing the efficacy and precision of melanoma identification.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), excluding other common skin lesion types (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma) present in the ISIC2019 dataset, which limits its clinical applicability for general dermatological screening.
The dataset size is small (3,297 images total; 2,637 training, 660 testing), raising concerns about the robustness and generalizability of the model to larger, more diverse populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the held-out test set of the ISIC2019 dataset, limiting evidence of generalizability to other institutions or imaging devices.
The paper acknowledges the need for data augmentation to address imbalanced data distribution but does not report using it in this specific experiment, potentially introducing bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the performance differences between the proposed XGBoost model and other classifiers.
The input images were resized to a low resolution of 64x64 pixels, which may result in the loss of fine-grained diagnostic details present in original dermoscopic images.

The paper is a conference publication (PuneCon 2023) with limited peer review scope compared to major journals, and the comparative analysis relies on metrics reported in other papers rather than direct re-evaluation on the same data.

The proposed method combines deep features with handcrafted RI-LBP features, but the specific contribution of each component to the final performance is not isolated via ablation studies in the provided text.
